title: The rescaled bootstrap
text: vignette demonstrates basic functionality surveybootstrap package. goal illustrate use rescaled bootstrap (Rao Wu 1988; Rust Rao 1996) take bootstrap resamples survey dataset survey sample complex design. rescaled bootstrap handles complex designs resampling level primary sampling unit (PSU) adjusting sampling weights every observation bootstrap replicate. Specifically, bootstrap resample, draw mi=ni−1m_i = n_i - 1 PSUs replacement nin_i PSUs stratum ii, rescales observation weights reflect many times PSU selected. package implements rescaled bootstrap C++ (via Rcpp) speed. Use rescaled.bootstrap.sample / bootstrap.estimates survey cluster stratified design. simple random sample without clusters strata, use srs.bootstrap.sample instead.

title: The rescaled bootstrap
text: MU284 dataset (Sarndal, Swensson, Wretman 2003, pg. 652) describes 284 Swedish municipalities. Relevant columns include: MU284.complex.surveys list 10 sample surveys drawn population using two-stage design Example 4.3.2 Sarndal et al.: Stage — simple random sample without replacement nI=5n_I = 5 PSUs (clusters) NI=50N_I = 50 total PSUs Stage II — simple random sample without replacement ni=3n_i = 3 municipalities selected PSU gives n=15n = 15 sampled municipalities per survey. row sample_weight reflecting inverse selection probability. two ways use package: Path 1: specifying function produce estimate data bootstrap weights Path 2: getting matrix bootstrap weights

title: The rescaled bootstrap
text: bootstrap.estimates() highest-level interface. supply estimator function bootstrap method; returns bootstrap replicate estimates can use compute standard errors confidence intervals.

title: The rescaled bootstrap
text: estimator must accept two arguments — survey.data (resampled dataset) weights (vector rescaled sampling weights) — return named data frame one row. total_S82 Horvitz-Thompson estimator population total S82 (total council seats), ratio_RMT85_P85 ratio estimator (tax revenue per capita).
code: my_estimator <- function(survey.data, weights) {   data.frame(     total_S82       = sum(survey.data$S82  * weights),     ratio_RMT85_P85 = sum(survey.data$RMT85 * weights) /                       sum(survey.data$P85   * weights)   ) }

title: The rescaled bootstrap
text: survey.design formula uses column CL PSU identifier. explicit strata , entire sample treated single stratum. See Describing survey design formulas strata. boot_res list 500 one-row data frames, one per bootstrap replicate.
code: set.seed(12345)  boot_res <- bootstrap.estimates(   survey.data   = survey,   survey.design = ~ CL,            # CL identifies the PSU (cluster)   num.reps      = 500,   estimator.fn  = my_estimator,    # we just defined this above   weights       = "sample_weight",   bootstrap.fn  = "rescaled.bootstrap.sample" )

title: The rescaled bootstrap
text: can also pass summary.fn bootstrap.estimates() get summary computed single pass without keeping replicates memory:
code: boot_df <- do.call("rbind", boot_res)  # Bootstrap standard errors apply(boot_df, 2, sd) #>       total_S82 ratio_RMT85_P85  #>    1638.2395064       0.8185707  # Bootstrap means (should be close to the original point estimates) apply(boot_df, 2, mean) #>       total_S82 ratio_RMT85_P85  #>    15290.525000        8.367827  # Original point estimates for comparison my_estimator(survey, survey$sample_weight) #>   total_S82 ratio_RMT85_P85 #> 1     15400        8.765395 boot_summary <- bootstrap.estimates(   survey.data   = survey,   survey.design = ~ CL,   num.reps      = 500,   estimator.fn  = my_estimator,   weights       = "sample_weight",   bootstrap.fn  = "rescaled.bootstrap.sample",   summary.fn    = MU284.estimator.summary.fn,  # example summary function   verbose       = FALSE )

title: The rescaled bootstrap
code: hist(boot_df$total_S82,      main  = "Bootstrap distribution: total council seats",      xlab  = "Estimated total (S82)",      col   = "steelblue",      border = "white") abline(v = my_estimator(survey, survey$sample_weight)$total_S82,        col = "firebrick", lwd = 2, lty = 2) legend("topright", legend = "Point estimate", col = "firebrick",        lwd = 2, lty = 2, bty = "n")

title: The rescaled bootstrap
text: Sometimes convenient obtain matrix bootstrap weights directly apply estimator(s) . useful : want compute many different estimators set replicates need pass bootstrap weights external software implementing jackknife--bootstrap (JAB) variance estimation

title: The rescaled bootstrap
text: result list two elements: orig_weights — data frame one row per respondent weight column containing original sampling weight boot_weights — data frame one row per respondent columns LABEL, boot_weight_1, …, boot_weight_500
code: boot_wts <- get.rescaled.bootstrap.weights(   survey.data   = survey,   survey.design = ~ CL,   idvar         = "LABEL",   weights       = "sample_weight",   num.reps      = 500 ) names(boot_wts) #> [1] "orig_weights" "boot_weights"  # First few rows and columns of boot_weights boot_wts$boot_weights[1:5, 1:4] #>   LABEL boot_weight_1 boot_weight_2 boot_weight_3 #> 1    96             0      66.66667      33.33333 #> 2    99             0      66.66667      33.33333 #> 3   101             0      66.66667      33.33333 #> 4    16             0      20.83333      20.83333 #> 5    18             0      20.83333      20.83333

title: The rescaled bootstrap
text: Join survey data weight matrix, apply estimator replicate:
code: # Merge survey data with bootstrap weights on the respondent id bw <- merge(survey[, c("LABEL", "S82", "RMT85", "P85")],             boot_wts$boot_weights,             by = "LABEL")  # Compute the total_S82 estimator for each bootstrap replicate boot_totals <- sapply(paste0("boot_weight_", 1:500), function(col) {   sum(bw$S82 * bw[[col]]) })  # Bootstrap standard error sd(boot_totals) #> [1] 1607.395

title: The rescaled bootstrap
text: Pass include_scaling_factors = TRUE also get per-replicate weight scaling factors. bootstrap weight product original weight corresponding scaling factor: boot_weighti,r=orig_weighti×boot_repi,r\text{boot\_weight}_{,r} = \text{orig\_weight}_i \times \text{boot\_rep}_{,r}
code: boot_wts_sf <- get.rescaled.bootstrap.weights(   survey.data            = survey,   survey.design          = ~ CL,   idvar                  = "LABEL",   weights                = "sample_weight",   num.reps               = 500,   include_scaling_factors = TRUE ) # boot_wts_sf$weight_scaling_factor has columns LABEL, boot_rep_1, ..., boot_rep_500

title: The rescaled bootstrap
text: survey.design argument formula tells surveybootstrap columns identify PSUs strata. simple random sample, use bootstrap.fn = "srs.bootstrap.sample" survey.design = ~ 1:
code: srs_survey <- MU284.surveys[[1]]  boot_srs <- bootstrap.estimates(   survey.data   = srs_survey,   survey.design = ~ 1,   num.reps      = 500,   estimator.fn  = my_estimator,   weights       = "sample_weight",   bootstrap.fn  = "srs.bootstrap.sample" )

title: bootstrap.estimates — bootstrap.estimates

title: bootstrap.estimates — bootstrap.estimates
code: # example using a simple random sample  survey <- MU284.surveys[[1]]  estimator <- function(survey.data, weights) {   plyr::summarise(survey.data,                   T82.hat = sum(S82 * weights)) }  ex.mu284 <- bootstrap.estimates(    survey.design = ~1,    num.reps = 10,    estimator.fn = estimator,    weights='sample_weight',    bootstrap.fn = 'srs.bootstrap.sample',    survey.data=survey)  if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ idu.est <- bootstrap.estimates(   ## this describes the sampling design of the   ## survey; here, the PSUs are given by the   ## variable cluster, and the strata are given   ## by the variable region   survey.design = ~ cluster + strata(region),   ## the number of bootstrap resamples to obtain   num.reps=1000,   ## this is the name of the function   ## we want to use to produce an estimate   ## from each bootstrapped dataset   estimator.fn="our.estimator",   ## these are the sampling weights   weights="indweight",   ## this is the name of the type of bootstrap   ## we wish to use   bootstrap.fn="rescaled.bootstrap.sample",   ## our dataset   survey.data=example.survey,   ## other parameters we need to pass   ## to the estimator function   d.hat.vals=d.hat,   total.popn.size=tot.pop.size,   y.vals="clients",   missing="complete.obs")  } # }

title: rescaled.bootstrap.weights — get.rescaled.bootstrap.weights
code: get.rescaled.bootstrap.weights(   survey.data,   survey.design,   idvar,   weights = NULL,   parallel = FALSE,   paropts = NULL,   num.reps = 1,   include_scaling_factors = FALSE,   include_cc = FALSE )

title: rescaled.bootstrap.weights — get.rescaled.bootstrap.weights
text: survey.data dataset use survey.design formula describing design survey (see Details) idvar name column survey.data respondent id weights Survey weights rescaled (NULL, none) parallel TRUE, use parallelization (via plyr) paropts optional list arguments passed plyr control details parallelization num.reps number bootstrap replication samples draw include_scaling_factors TRUE, include weight_scaling_factor output include_cc TRUE, include cluster counts output. cluster counts needed jackknife bootstrap calculations

title: rescaled.bootstrap.weights — get.rescaled.bootstrap.weights
code: A list with entries:  * `orig_weights` - A dataframe with rows corresponding to the original    `survey.data` and a column with the original (un rescaled) weight for the obs.    Can be useful for debugging.  * `boot_weights` - A dataframe with rows corresponding to the original    `survey.data` and one column for each bootstrap rep. The entries have    the rescaled bootstrap weights for each row and bootstrap rep.  * `weight_scaling_factor` - (if `include_scaling_factors` is `TRUE`). A dataframe with rows corresponding to the original    `survey.data` and one column for each bootstrap rep. The entries have    the values by which the original weights were scaled to produce `boot_weights`.    You typically won't need to use this dataframe, but it can be helpful for debugging.  * `cluster_counts` - (if `include_cc` is `TRUE`). A list with `num.reps` entries.   Each entry is a dataset which has the variable '.cluster_id',   the values of columns that specify the PSU (from the `survey.design` formula),   and `cluster_count` (the number of times the given PSU was resampled))

title: rescaled.bootstrap.weights — get.rescaled.bootstrap.weights
text: new function added part refactor v.0.2. function focuses able return dataframe bootstrap weights, optionally also dataframe cluster/PSU inclusion counts. survey.design formula form weight ~ psu_vars + strata(strata_vars) : weight variable survey weights psu_vars form psu_v1 + psu_v2 + ..., primary sampling units (PSUs) determined psu_v1, etc strata_vars form strata_v1 + strata_v2 + ..., determine strata Note assume formula uniquely specifies PSUs. always true PSUs selected without replacement. selected replacement, necessary make realization given PSU sample unique id. code assumes observations within PSU (identified design formula) draw PSU. rescaled bootstrap technique works adjusting estimation weights based number times row included resamples. row never selected, still included returned results, weight set 0. therefore important use estimators make use estimation weights resampled datasets. always take m_i = n_i - 1, according advice presented Rao Wu (1988) Rust Rao (1996). (C++ version; previous version, written pure R, called rescaled.bootstrap.sample.pureR() ) References: Rust, Keith F., J. N. K. Rao. "Variance estimation complex surveys using replication techniques." Statistical methods medical research 5.3 (1996): 283-310. Rao, Jon NK, C. F. J. Wu. "Resampling inference complex survey data." Journal American Statistical Association 83.401 (1988): 231-241.

title: rescaled.bootstrap.sample — rescaled.bootstrap.sample
text: Given survey dataset description survey design (ie, combination variables determines primary sampling units, combination variables determines strata), take bunch bootstrap samples rescaled bootstrap estimator (see Details).

title: rescaled.bootstrap.sample — rescaled.bootstrap.sample
code: rescaled.bootstrap.sample(   survey.data,   survey.design,   parallel = FALSE,   paropts = NULL,   num.reps = 1,   include_cc = FALSE )

title: rescaled.bootstrap.sample — rescaled.bootstrap.sample
text: survey.data dataset use survey.design formula describing design survey (see Details) parallel TRUE, use parallelization (via plyr) paropts optional list arguments passed plyr control details parallelization num.reps number bootstrap replication samples draw include_cc TRUE, include cluster counts output. cluster counts needed jackknife bootstrap calculations

title: rescaled.bootstrap.sample — rescaled.bootstrap.sample
code: The return value will depend on whether or not you ask for the cluster counts (using `include_cc`). If `include_cc` is `FALSE` (the default), return   A list with `num.reps` entries. Each entry is a dataset which   has at least the variables `index` (the row index of the original   dataset that was resampled) and `weight.scale`   (the factor by which to multiply the sampling weights   in the original dataset).   If `include_cc` is `TRUE`, then return a list with two entries:  * `weight_scaling_factor` - A list with `num.reps` entries.    Each entry is a dataset which   has at least the variables `index` (the row index of the original   dataset that was resampled) and `weight.scale`   (the factor by which to multiply the sampling weights   in the original dataset). * `cluster_counts` - A list with `num.reps` entries.   Each entry is a dataset which has the variable '.cluster_id',   the values of columns that specify the PSU (from the `survey.design` formula),   and `cluster_count` (the number of times the given PSU was resampled))

title: rescaled.bootstrap.sample — rescaled.bootstrap.sample
text: survey.design formula form weight ~ psu_vars + strata(strata_vars) : weight variable survey weights psu_vars form psu_v1 + psu_v2 + ..., primary sampling units (PSUs) determined psu_v1, etc strata_vars form strata_v1 + strata_v2 + ..., determine strata Note assume formula uniquely specifies PSUs. always true PSUs selected without replacement. selected replacement, necessary make realization given PSU sample unique id. code assumes observations within PSU (identified design formula) draw PSU. rescaled bootstrap technique works adjusting estimation weights based number times row included resamples. row never selected, still included returned results, weight set 0. therefore important use estimators make use estimation weights resampled datasets. always take m_i = n_i - 1, according advice presented Rao Wu (1988) Rust Rao (1996). (C++ version; previous version, written pure R, called rescaled.bootstrap.sample.pureR() ) References: Rust, Keith F., J. N. K. Rao. "Variance estimation complex surveys using replication techniques." Statistical methods medical research 5.3 (1996): 283-310. Rao, Jon NK, C. F. J. Wu. "Resampling inference complex survey data." Journal American Statistical Association 83.401 (1988): 231-241.

title: rescaled.bootstrap.sample — rescaled.bootstrap.sample

title: rescaled.bootstrap.sample.pureR — rescaled.bootstrap.sample.pureR
text: given survey dataset description survey design (ie, combination vars determines primary sampling units, combination vars determines strata), take bunch bootstrap samples rescaled bootstrap estimator (see, eg, Rust Rao 1996). Note assume formula uniquely specifies PSUs. always true PSUs selected without replacement. selected replacement, necessary make realization given PSU sample unique id. Bottom line: code assumes observations within PSU (identified design formula) draw PSU. rescaled bootstrap technique works adjusting estimation weights based number times row included resamples. row never selected, still included returned results, weight set 0. therefore important use estimators make use estimation weights resampled datasets. always take m_i = n_i - 1, according advice presented Rao Wu (1988) Rust Rao (1996). survey.design formula form weight ~ psu_vars + strata(strata_vars), weight variable survey weights psu variable denoting primary sampling unit

title: rescaled.bootstrap.weights — rescaled.bootstrap.weights
code: survey <- MU284.complex.surveys[[1]] rescaled.bootstrap.weights(survey.data = survey,                           survey.design = ~ CL,                           weights='sample_weight',                           idvar='LABEL',                           num.reps = 2) #>    LABEL boot_weight_1 boot_weight_2 #> 1     96             0      33.33333 #> 2     99             0      33.33333 #> 3    101             0      33.33333 #> 4     16             0      20.83333 #> 5     18             0      20.83333 #> 6     19             0      20.83333 #> 7    221             0       0.00000 #> 8    223             0       0.00000 #> 9    224             0       0.00000 #> 10   188            50       0.00000 #> 11   190            50       0.00000 #> 12   191            50       0.00000 #> 13   199            50      50.00000 #> 14   200            50      50.00000 #> 15   203            50      50.00000   if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ bootweights <- rescaled.bootstrap.weights(                                          # formula describing survey design:                                          # psu and strata                                          survey.design = ~ psu +                                                            stratum(stratum_analysis),                                          num.reps=10000,                                          # column with respondent ids                                          idvar='caseid',                                          # column with sampling weight                                          weights='wwgt',                                          # survey dataset                                          survey.data=mw.ego)  } # }

title: surveybootstrap 0.2.0
text: Added get.rescaled.bootstrap.weights(): new function returns rescaled bootstrap weights (optionally cluster counts scaling factors) wide data frame, making straightforward use rescaled bootstrap jackknife--bootstrap (JAB) variance estimation rescaled.bootstrap.sample() gains include_cc argument; TRUE returns per-rep cluster inclusion counts alongside weight-scaling factors, needed JAB calculations Fixed bug bootstrap.estimates() failed silently used rescaled.bootstrap.sample(): weight-scaling column named weight_scale (underscore) sampler read weight.scale (dot) estimator, causing weights collapse numeric(0) estimates NaN / Inf Expanded test suite: added structural semantic tests rescaled.bootstrap.sample(), srs.bootstrap.sample(), get.rescaled.bootstrap.weights(), rescaled.bootstrap.weights(); added regression test directly catches weight.scale naming bug; added tests weighted.mean() helper

title: surveybootstrap 0.0.3
text: CRAN release: 2023-01-09 fixed dependency errors suddenly arose (explicitly use plyr:: places) dropped now-deprecated group_indices_() favor cur_group_index() changed docs use Markdown roxygen added MU284 dataset examples tests added examples exported function docs

title: surveybootstrap 0.0.2
text: moved unit tests goc helper functions networkreporting added rescaled.bootstrap.weights helper function case tibble rescaled bootstrap weights useful added dependencies tidyverse, magrittr, purrr

title: surveybootstrap 0.0.1
